segments.text: So for half an hour
I'm about to sit up here with my gloves.
Right. Good morning. So,
we've got you for about half an hour.
Yeah.
And we thought we'd
give you 10 minutes or so to talk about
the bill and your intentions, and then there'll be some questions.
Okay. Well, look, thank you very much, committee members, for allowing me to come
along. I just want to step aside
from
the kind of political frost at the moment
and take us to the core of the bill, what I think
I was doing.
And I want you to please believe, I go back on this
issue to about 1986,
'87, '88, when I first got involved with
where
industry meets environment, meets Maori Rights
Tribunal. So that, quite apart from my upbringing up near the 90 Mile
Beach, that was my first
introduction to where the different forces
meet each other in the fishery sector.
So,
most of what's in this bill, Mr. Chairman, other
governments have sought to do.
The thing that laid me low on the undersized fish,
it
was in the bill, if I can start there, because that's what's got me into trouble.
If I can start there. It was in the bill after my dear friend,
David Parker,
introduced it and put it in an earlier version of fisheries
legislation.
And on that question, what I thought I was doing, as a number of you have
hammered me for it,
is that if harvesters who have
quota catch fish,
either intentionally, unintentionally,
they should be required to bring it back to shore and it should come
off their quota.
Obviously, that's now canceled and dead after the Prime Minister
and various others responded to the viral campaign.
But from an international fisheries perspective,
which is why David Parker, because I rang him and asked him, "Why the hell did you
do this in the first place?" He said, "Well, Shane, I was advised internationally
that is the best way to change the behavior of
harvesters and penalize them because it will come off their legal
entitlement."
So, that's now gone.
So,
it probably wasn't well explained by me, but once, Mr.
Chair, it went viral, then we all piled in on it.
And then I met with the PM and Winston, that was it.
But I want everyone to know, there was actually a rationale to it.
Right.
So, if I can put that to the side. That's dead.
On the question of the cameras, and I want to address my colleagues on the Green
Party, for me, it's a constitutional principle
that the state is responsible for
undertaking enforcement action,
prosecutions,
or using the force of law to
penalize
or to take people into our legal courts.
It is not the NGOs. It is not the public.
So, I just want you to understand, that's philosophically what I've
explained to my colleagues.
And there's one other area I'd like to cover off, and then I'll just take
questions.
And look, I'm fully aware, Mr. Chair,
the bill won't pass unless it has a majority of votes.
But I do feel we do need to pass a bill of some
sorts because there are some legislative cliffs, which no
doubt, when I've gone, you can talk to the officials about.
However, the final other area which seems to have got
everyone highly agitated
is the ability
to carry an entitlement forward over multiple
years,
which you can do at the margin under the current law.
And we did look at doing this under COVID, for
example. That if there was disfigurement
in the capacity of people to catch fish,
Jacinda and us looked at whether or not you should carry the quota forward
into another year.
So, what I have put on the bill, or have got the
government to agree to put on the bill, is the capacity for us to
carry quota forward, and it still will be in
a set of guardrails that are defined by the
purpose of the act.
I unashamedly say to you that a lot of these ideas
have been driven by me because of my
concerns about productivity, efficiency,
contributions to enabling the industry to evolve,
which is an industry based on property rights.
I have a kind of war of words going with the
recreational sector.
But I want to say to you, nothing in this bill inhibits a
recreational man or woman from catching fish.
And we have the most permissive regime in the world for
recreational fisheries. We do not require
reportingWe do not even fully know
how much fish is taken out of the ocean by the recreational sector.
We have a model, but that model, I can tell you to the last
kilo how much fish that Sanford's catch.
Cannot tell you how much recreationals catch.
So they are defending an incredibly liberal and permissive regime,
and I suspect that in the future, another government will have to look at that.
But it's gunpowder for this minister, so I'll just park that here.
So anyway, that's my opinion.
Thanks, Minister. We'll go to questions. Uh, Teanau.
Yeah. Mōrena. My question is around
access to advice, access to information, so OIAs.
So as we know-
Yes
... in order for us to have a really good discussion, and I know
certainly for different people that have put in submissions, they'll want to have
the most up-to-date information.
We've had requests go in there that's past the 20-day
frame, and groups like, say, Legacy, are sort of wanting
to get that stuff. So my question is, will you
instruct your officials to release that?
Yeah. Okay, if I just start with
obstructing
the ability of people,
under the Official Information Act, to download the
camera footage.
Yeah.
This is why I say the camera footage can be made available for academic
study.
This is an OIA on advice to official services.
Oh, okay. Well, I'll work out what we're allowed to share.
Yeah.
I really don't care.
Okay, cool.
I'll just find out from Ray Smith and Emma
what the convention is. I'm not dodging your question.
Yeah, no.
And I'll just-
I just probably-
Yeah, I'll just work out with them the availability of
advice. Most of the advice I get, I spout it on the front page of the paper anyway.
I know. That's why people-
... want to have it.
But I don't want to put her on the...
Put Ray-
I'm happy to respond if that's-
Oh, fine. Yes, please.
So I understand there are quite a large number of OIAs with the ministry at the
moment, and most of the advice around that has informed the bill has
been made public already. But there are some additional
sort of briefings and advice that have been, I think that's going through its final
stages and should be released shortly.
How shortly is short? Is that days, months?
I'll need to check in with the team, but I imagine within the next few days, so we
can-
So next few days. He's keen to get it out.
We can update the committee when we have next committee this afternoon.
Okay.
I'll check on it. Yeah.
Okay.
Rachel.
Thanks, Chair. Thanks, Minister. I've got a few lines of questioning.
Yeah, fire away.
So stop me when you feel you need to go to our members because we've got time.
So happy to kind of begin.
I've got two big concerns with the bill. I'll just put that up front.
And so I guess what I'd like to hear from you is where do you see the potential
changes in order to actually get some support around the parliament?
Because at this stage, if the bill looks like it does now,
it's
probably not going to pass in its next pace.
The two big pieces-
I have told my national friends the price will go up on the November 9th.
So
the two concerns I want to raise that
I'd love a response on,
the first is around judicial review.
And so I'll just-
Okay
... because I don't think that the changes made are
reasonable because actually, in New Zealand, we have an
approach where people can take judicial review.
I appreciate that it adds cost to industry, I appreciate it adds cost to
government, but the ability to do a judicial review on the
implementation of the act is actually an important part of our democracy.
Second question I've got, I'll just put the bloke up, is around the carry
forward. That's where I have my biggest concern,
and I appreciate that one of the things around fish stocks is
sometimes actually the interaction between the two.
If you think about snapper and kina, for example, is a great example of where
you've got one depleting and the other one growing, which then causes other issues.
So there is some benefit in having flexibility
around... I actually do appreciate that.
However, my concern is that we are getting good data
now on what's being taken.
I'm not convinced we still have good data on what is actually out there in the
waters that's still there.
And I don't think our data's good enough for us to be making some of these
decisions around carry forward. It concerns me that if quota has
been built up
over time
and not used, that then it allows a large
amount to be taken, and that could have a
massive impact on the fishery stock.
So I'm just not convinced we're there yet in terms of data.
So I'd just love a response from you.
Yeah, okay. Let's start with the High Court.
The Ministry of Justice do not agree
with what I advocated for in cabinet.
And God knows if there's an MP over his lifetime who's used the inherent
jurisdiction of the High Court to stop the state from doing things, it's me.
But I do think that
on the fisheries question,
it's become a blood sport.
And what I was trying to do was put some guardrails
around and some time frames
so that people, when they did challenge egregious cases of bad
decision-making,
that
it wasn't open-ended. Because at the moment,
I don't think it's reasonable that
Eli can reprise a decision from three or four years ago,
which is happening. And I'd rather that...
We did a little bit in the fast track, but not really.
And I accept that it's something that probably the
constitutional purists will want to change anyway.
But just going to be upfront with you, I don't want to block off the inherent
jurisdiction access questions to the High
CourtBut one day you guys are gonna be in
government. You really do not want what I'm seeing as a
proliferation of
litigants, basically weaponizing the
law as a basis for addressing a host of issues
that have very often nothing to do with
fisheries.
And that burden falls on the taxpayer, and then that burden is
often picked up by the industry. That's my philosophy.
But to your cardinal point, but shame you're defeating a
constitutional tenet. Mm-hmm. Maybe that's because
my original suggestion, which as you know, because you can see the
briefings, they don't like it,
maybe needs to be tempered. But I would plead with you to think about the
principle
of ensuring that when we allow
ongoing challenges to the
legitimacy of a statutory decision-maker,
that it's within some bloody air of reasonableness.
That's what I think. In relation to the
quota.
Look, Jim Anderton tried to do this in Helen's time.
Helen came in in '99,
and for a period of time,
Jim towards the end there. So this is
not novel, and I plead with you, go and talk to the people in Nelson.
Oh, I have been, don't you worry. And they'll tell you
that
it is an overdue innovation that's needed in the industry.
It is not going to compromise the total
allowable commercial catch, which is a percentage of the
total allowable catch.
And
is it something that I think is worth fighting for?
Yeah, I do.
Do I think that it represents a threat
to the sustainability of a stock? No, I don't.
But I fully accept that you should prosecute that issue with Emma and the
officials during the course of your hearings.
You had a sup.
Yeah, just a sup on the judicial review thing.
You were saying that we think that the committee should
think about tempering my point, and I take that point.
But we were just talking earlier around the concerns around the
release of official information.
And so that advice,
in this case, has taken more than 20 days.
So if you
restrict judicial review down to that 20 days, by the time you get useful
information, you can't use it.
I hear what you're saying about the balancing point, but- Well, Eva's come up with
a timeframe.
Emma will show you what the Ministry of Justice told me.
They almost had a stroke when they saw what I had ended up doing as this bill.
There's no reason why
we can't provide you with that information.
And I don't want anyone in this room thinking that
I'm negating what is a key feature of
our Westminster democracy, the inherent jurisdiction of the High
Court. I'm just asking you, one day you guys are going to be where I am,
to build some guardrails around it. That's all I'm asking.
And just one other thing around the
whistleblower kind of provisions, that if you do release information, you
get pinged up to $50,000. So you think that's a-
That's over the top, eh?
Yeah. Well-
By the way, they do as well.
Yeah. Okay. So
what's happening there and that's a
bit of a-
Oh, well. How does Donald Trump negotiate? Starts way up there and ends over here.
All right.
Yeah. But
when we
introduced the ear tag scheme,
you-
NAIT.
NAIT, yeah. You would remember
that there are strong protections about the privacy
of that information. That is currently in the law.
And what I wanted was to protect
the information from being used for purposes
which I do not believe are related or legitimate to fishing.
Mm. So therefore, it's the state who prosecutes my nephew when he's
smoking dope. It's the state who hauls
off my young fella for driving drunk.
Although a photo may have been taken of him at Coopers Beach and put up on
Facebook, that's the state's role.
And I can just see a period of time where a lot of this
footage, and no other industry, folks, has this level of
surveillance. No other industry.
As you know, philosophically, I'm opposed to it anyway, but I got overruled by
Cabinet, so that's now history. So if the state
has required this level of mass surveillance on boats,
then it's the state's role to guard that information and to use it to
effect the purposes of the statute.
Mm.
Good enough for the NAIT users, it's good enough for the fishing industry.
Sue.
You talked just briefly before about legislative cliffs,
that-
Pardon?
You talked about legislative cliffs.
Yeah.
That some of the legislation needs to be passed-
Yeah
... to avoid legislative cliffs, and I just wondered how many of them there are and
where might they reside?
I'll get Emma.
I'm not making it up. There generally are some.
Yeah.
But I'll get
Emma, who's the most senior person, given that Mr.
Bulger is away for a while.
They'll just give you a list of them.
And
if we don't tidy it up,
the Prime Minister and my leader and David Seymour said, "Right, we're
not bringing undersized fish back to shore.
That is not happening." Okay? If we don't pass a bill, it will be
happening.
You with me?
Yeah.
Simple as that. Now, I want it to happen.
Mm.
But it's the industry who's very happy
that the recs and the greens and all my other millions of
opponentsStopped undersized fish, including the
National Party. But what they don't realize, they've given the industry exactly
what they wanted all along.
They do not want to bring undersized fish back and have it come
off their quota.
But I spoke with the PM last night, told him I was coming.
He said, "Shane,
we are not reversing that decision.
I don't care about the rationality of what you and David Parker thought you were
doing. That's a done deal."
Rachel.
Thanks, Chair. I guess I just want to make, I guess, an
observation and a plea, which is around how we approach
this discussion. Because I think,
last night, great event led by Live Ocean, and a range of
views from industry through to iwi through to
NGOs.
Mm-hmm.
And I guess I just put to you, can you accept that I think everyone
involved in this conversation actually wants an abundant fishery
that can be used for commercial purposes and for the purposes
of individuals to be able to feed their whanau?
Yep.
And this is actually a plea to everybody
involved on all sides of the debate, that when we use inflammatory language
or don't meet with people who
are interested in this, that actually it sets us all
apart into a polarized conversation.
And can we actually do our best through this process to really
listen to the concerns and dive into the actual intent and the data?
That's my plea and question to you.
Yeah. I think that's fair enough. Let us, Steve.
Thank you, Mr. Chair. Just on the
camera footage, Minister.
The reason for such extensive surveillance in
commercial fishing is because of a long history
of breaches and exceedances of limits,
exceedances of law, dumping, all sorts of
questionable practices. Hauling up coral, and you name it.
Was it not for that footage-
Mm-hmm
there would be no evidential basis for either the state prosecuting
breaches or for the public to even know, because,
as we've seen from previous releases, reporting on
breaches was not accurate on the part of the commercial
industry.
Uh-huh.
So the question is, if this is publicly owned footage
belonging to the state,
why can the public not utilize it or see it to
understand what's going on in the fishery, including
advocates for better enforcement of the law and
advocates for protection of what is, after all, a commons?
Well, I
kind of agree with most of what you're saying, Steve,
but it comes down to trust.
Okay. I don't trust a lot of your fellow travelers.
Obviously, I trust you as a parliament. I'll be straight up with you.
I do not trust a lot of your fellow travelers.
I trust-
They probably don't trust you either, Minister.
Pardon?
They probably don't trust you either.
Well, Gary Taylor,
we agree to disagree. Russell has a stroke every time he sees
me. But put all that to the side.
What I'm asking, Steve, for you to consider is that we should
trust,
constitutionally, the people we put in a
position of power
to enforce through the powers of the state,
prosecutorial
or
legal decisions.
I don't think it's fair to my fellow New Zealanders that
indiscriminate footage be made available,
then weaponized,
then taken out of context
and unfairly used.
Because I would say the same thing should happen to his industry,
the cow industry, the calf industry.
And
I think that I accept the
coverage
changes behavior, but I'm asking you to,
as a parliamentarian, stay with the model.
The enforcement of the law in the country rests with the cops
and the regulators. Does not rest with Russell, doesn't rest with New
Zealand First. That's how our constitutional system works.
That, at the pith of what I'm saying, is probably
the hill that I'd die on.
Teanau.
Yeah. Actually, it was a sub on what Rachel was saying.
And I thought, well, because it comes down to social
license. In order for anything to happen, right, you've got to have enough people
in the country to be able to support that, and that's right across the fishery.
Yeah.
And I was at that event last night, and there are very strong
concerns around bottom trawling. Strong concerns.
People are opposed to bottom trawling.
So how's the balance going to happen, given that the social license
around that has evaporated?
Okay. So can I put it another way?
You and I, as Maori,
we don't have a social license, we have rights.
And I'm not surrendering those rights.
Those rights are the full and final settlement after a century and a
half of colonization.
I don't care about these modern innovations of social license.
I've got rights,
and I feel that social license is often used as a
convenient way
of smothering or circumventing the fact that there are rights.
So philosophically, that's where I come from.
Secondly, I had Jono and Blair in my
office yesterday, and I told the staff, "Look, whatever information,
unless I'm breaking the law, just give it all to them."In terms of the
granularity of what peak around the Chathams we're finding,
this is obviously a major surprise
to EMA. But if we're going to have a debate for the next
five, 10 years,
then let it be based on facts.
Mm.
And the fact that I put to you as committee members, 70% of the
fish in New Zealand is currently caught by
purse seining or dredging.
So, I don't think it's fair
to use social license
as a basis for smudging out
70% of an industry.
But
it is an easy out, as I told Blair and Jono.
I told Jono to go into politics, by the way. "You're 36 years old.
Stop swimming against the tide and hop into politics and go with the
tide."
And he probably thought, "Who's that fat old guy telling me what to do?"
So, I guess I'd say, Chair, that
I just cannot agree, guys. I
just cannot agree
that, um-
It's probably more a how you navigate it.
Pardon?
We're parliamentarians. We've got to... You know what I mean?
Yeah, but it just seems to me that-
No, I hear what you're saying
... if we want to stop, as I said, look, if we want to do stop
that fishing practice and those harvesting techniques, and I accept that there's a
burgeoning desire to do that,
then get ready on behalf of society to
purchase the rights back,
because we're making a moral judgment, some unseen coral
at some point in the ocean is of more importance to the
rights that we have. And I don't agree with you.
I just do not agree that some obscure coral has more moral
significance than my rights.
Look, Minister, they're trashing the ocean though.
Yeah.
They're destroying the fishery for their own-
And that coral plays a-
Hang on
Yeah
... Steve.
Yeah, let's just agree to disagree.
I do not believe that the level of destruction in the
New Zealand fishing industry
is anywhere at the catastrophic level that
Attenborough and all these other do-gooders depict it as.
And if that's the standard that we're going to run, then let's put
all the Kahikatea forests of the Hauraki Plains back.
Well, there's a point you can't put them back once they're gone.
Yeah.
That's why we're trying to stop it.
No, of course you can put it back.
We're trying to stop it-
You can close down farming and you can plant a trillion trees
And wait 1,000 years.
Exactly.
I've got to step on this because I just want to push back on that-
Last question
... which is,
the coral and what's on the bottom of the ocean plays a critical
role in actually ensuring the survivability of the stock.
And that's the point, is that if we do
continue to do the destruction, it-- And that's why I came back to that
comment I made, which was we all want a abundant fishery.
So I just have to push back. The role of the coral and what's on
the sea floor is important for spawning and ensuring we have
stock that we can go and fish to feed ourselves and the world.
But do you really believe, this is a great discussion,
does the Labour Party really believe
that we are presiding over an industry
which earns about $2 billion, so it's not an inordinately large
industry. Do you really believe that since
1986 we've been presiding over the
irreversible destruction of our sea floor?
I don't.
I think that that is actually a-
And I think it's been weaponized.
I disagree with you, and I think that if we continue down this path, that's when we
end up in a situation where we say, "Can we go back?" And if-
Oh, that's what we're going to-
And this is what we're here to discuss.
Yeah.
There is going to be consequences.
I'm sorry, we're running out of time.
Mm-hmm.
But, thank you very much for your time, Minister.
My feeling is
we should try and get the bill back into the House.
And then if it does help to pop along
and
at a more informal level to talk to my Labour colleagues, as
well colleagues, or I'm up for it.
Mm-hmm.
But if the bill is going to be so emasculated,
then what I think needs to happen, Mr.
Chair, is that, we need a bill of some sort because of certain
leaps of cliffs.
I'll be very disappointed because I regard
the fishing industry as being really important,
not only for food and protein, but as a key employer of
regional communities. And the ideas that I'm trying to
push forward, not all of them.
I take your point about judicial review.
They-- It's worth fighting for.
Thank you very much.
Okay, thanks.
Thank you.
Thank you, Minister.
Thank you.
Excellent. Right, we're going to move on quickly now.
We've got
New Zealand Meat Board
for half an hour.
So welcome, Nick, and welcome, Megan.
Morning, everyone.
Gidday.
Can you be as entertaining?
I was actually going to say, I'm sorry, because I won't be as entertaining.
Come on, now.
I did say to Megan, we should have actually come in here a lot earlier to answer to
that.
Look,
thanks very much and we'll leave it in your hands.
We've got half an hour, so
if you could leave some time for questions, that'd be really useful.
Yep, awesome. And good morning, and thanks for the opportunity.
My name's Nick Beebe, for those who I haven't met
I'm the chief executive of the New Zealand Meat Board.
And I'd like to start with a simple observation.
I've been working in this sector for about 25-odd years, and what I really
admire
is that New Zealand has always been constantly at the forefront of
agribusiness innovation around the world.
We've always been adaptable, pragmatic, outward-looking, and we've
always competed really successfully in some of the most high-value markets
around the world. But that success has never happened by accident.
It's enabled by systems, institutions, and decisions that were put
in place by those who came before us, by our parents and
grandparents, and earlier generations of industry leaders
who actually built the foundations that we continue to rely on today.
The New Zealand Meat Board is one of the clearest examples of that
legacy in action, and yet it remains something
of one of the best-kept secrets within the primary sector.
Many farmers that I speak to
are not fully aware of what the board does, and that's pretty
understandable because much of our work happens behind the
scenes. It happens at that interface between exporters,
global markets,
and governments, rather than directly on the farm itself.
But without the work that we do, the industry that we have today would look
very different.
And so to understand that, I'm just going to go back a little bit
in history, back to 1920,
They were extremely difficult years for New Zealand farmers.
The bulk buying arrangement with the United Kingdom that was
established during the First World War came to an end.
Product accumulated in storage, inventories rose, prices fell,
and a poor season compounded all that pressure.
Those years were a period of real uncertainty for our
farmers. But as we've seen time and time again in the sector, when
conditions get really tough, farmers respond.
And out of that period came collective action and a lot of foresight.
following swift legislative
change, the New Zealand Meat Board was established, and the first meeting was held
in Wellington of March that year.
At that stage, the Meat Board had representation from both farmers
and the government.
That decision, taken in response to a crisis, laid the
foundation for one of New Zealand's most successful export industries.
But over time, over the past 100-odd years,
the board's role has changed and evolved quite significantly.
In the early years, it was directly involved in marketing, promotion,
research and development, and a range of
activities on-farm, but also
within the meat plants as well.
And as the industry matured,
many of those functions shifted to other
organizations, particularly from the early 2000s,
to organizations such as Beef and Lamb New Zealand.
And so today, the system is far more specialized, with clear
roles across the sector. Government, industry bodies and
exporters focus on securing market access and negotiating trade agreements.
And once that access is secured,
the Meat Board steps in to implement and administer it.
That clarity of roles is important because it ensures clear focus and
reduces duplication and ultimately helps deliver
the best
possible returns to our farmers.
So today, the Meat Board focuses on three
core responsibilities, the first of which is to administer
quota.
We administer tariff rate quotas into high-value markets, which include the
US, the UK, and the EU. And these are
not abstract trade mechanisms. They are highly
valuable commercial assets.
Currently, the quotas we manage underpin more than $4.2
billion of export revenue, and annually we
deliver tariff rate savings of about $1.4
billion
per year. And that value flows directly back
through the supply chain and ultimately
underpins farmgate returns.
We now manage 10 quotas
for beef, sheep meat, and goat meat.
These include some of the older, more legacy WTO
agreements, but also the much newer
free trade agreements to the UK and the EU.
In the year to the 30th of September '25, export to these quota markets
increased by about 36%,
while tariff savings rose by about
60% compared to the previous year.
So these are very strong results, but they also
sit within a very
complex and volatile trading
environment.
Even in the last
18 months, we've seen really strong shifting demand
patterns, changing trade policies, and increasing geopolitical
uncertainty. So in that environment, the importance of these
secure, diversified market access arrangements
becomes
just actually critical.
An example of that, the weaker demand in China
over the past year,
that strength in our traditional markets has been critical.
High utilization of lamb quotas, strong demand for beef in the
US, and growing interest in the UK and the EU have helped
offset that weaker demand in China.So that's
the value of the flexibility
that we provide.
What New Zealand's current allocation provides-- so within China, some
of those developments include the recent beef
safeguard investigation and the new quota settings, and
that highlights the ongoing uncertainty in global trade.
And while New Zealand's
current allocation provides some headroom in the short term in China,
it also reinforces the need for constant monitoring and the ability to respond
quickly as conditions change.
So overall quota utilization strengthened in 2025 and has
remained strong in '26.
The second core area of responsibility for the Meat Board
is around the management of farmer reserves.
And I think this is another really good example of some of that long-term thinking
in action. The reserves
originate from surplus funds that were accumulated during New Zealand's bulk
meat supply arrangements with the UK during the Second World War.
And today they stand
at more than NZ$98 million, and they are held as a
contingency fund, a sort of a form of insurance for
the sector. Their purpose is really specific, is to support the
industry to help re-enter export markets following a major
disruption such as a biosecurity incursion or a significant loss of
market access.
And the importance of that role is not
theoretical. Events such as foot and mouth outbreaks
internationally serve as a constant reminder of the risks that our
sector faces.
In the event of a major disruption, the ability to draw on these reserves
could have a significant impact on restoring market confidence and supporting the
recovery.
The third area that I just wanted to cover
briefly is how we use that income from the reserves
to fund industry good investment.
And this is, I suppose, where the Meat Board's work does connect directly
with what happens on farm.
The board itself does not design or run research
programs itself. That role is taken up with
industry good organizations
like Beef and Lamb New Zealand. Instead, we actually assess the proposals,
consult with farmers and allocate funding to initiatives that will deliver
long-term benefits for the sector.
Since 2004, the board has committed more than $80
million to industry good programs.
I believe these investments have supported some of the most important
developments in the sector over the past two decades.
Research into pastoral greenhouse gas
consortia, which provides New Zealand farmers with knowledge and tools to help
mitigate greenhouse gas emissions.
We also were a significant investor into the Red Meat Profit partnership,
which delivered tools and systems that are widely used
today, such as the electronic animal status declaration form
and the New Zealand Farm Assurance program.
They were components of the Red Meat Profit partnership that we invested in.
More recently, the board has been supporting the Eliminating Facial
Eczema Impacts program, and we contribute up to
$1.5 million per year to a seven-year initiative to help eliminate facial
eczema,
which is a significant animal health challenge.
We've also committed funding to the Informing New Zealand Beef program, which is
focused on lifting the performance of the national beef herd through improved
genetics.
These examples
illustrate an important point. The reserve fund allows the
sector to invest in areas where there is market failure, where the
benefits are widely shared, but the incentives for individual investment
are limited, and genetics is a great example of that.
Improving the genetics requires investment at the breeding level,
but the benefits accrue often further down the production chain.
So without that collective investment up front, progress can be
slower than it should be.
So by investing in the front end,
we're ensuring that the industry continues to lift the
productivity and capture higher value in global markets.
That includes supporting technologies that allow farmers to benchmark and
improve performance using world-class genomic and pedigree data.
These results have been transformational in the sheep sector and are now
being extended into beef.
All of this sits
within a strong statutory and governance framework.
The board operates under the Meat Board Act of 2004.
We're required to administer and manage quota systems that are efficient,
transparent and fully compliant with New Zealand's international
obligations. This means delivering systems that are accurate,
reliable and trusted by both exporters and our trading
partners, and this is often recognized
by our international trading partners.
It also means maintaining the integrity of New Zealand's quota access,
something that is critical to our international reputation and relationships.
Over the past year, we've taken a really detailed look at our systems
to ensure
that they provide the most integrity
and are incredibly resilient. So we're now rebuilding our quota
management platform to ensure it's modern, secure and fit for
the future.
We're also working with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade and the Ministry
of Primary Industries to improve our data sharing
with key partners such as the European.
None of this is really high profile.
A lot of this
sits behind the scenes, but it's actually critical to maintaining the credibility
and effectiveness of our
systems.The strength of the... Pretty much just
finally, the strength of the board also lies in our governance.
It brings together farmer-elected directors,
processor and exporter representatives, and government
appointees on the board. And these government appointees
are particularly important because they all bring deep expertise in
international
trade.
This combination ensures decisions are grounded in both practical
farming experience and providing a clear understanding of global
market dynamics.
So just really stepping back,
all the systems that we rely on today did not happen overnight.
A lot of them were grounded back into those very early years of
The Meat Board is part of the legacy of the red meat
industry, and that continuity matters, because while
the context changes, whether it be shifting consumer preferences
or the real global geopolitical tensions that we see or the
biosecurity risks that may happen, the need for strong trusted
systems remains constant.
So look, I'm just going to leave it
there. I just wanted to
provide a bit of a background into the New Zealand Meat Board because
as I said, it's not really that well understood, but plays
a critical role within our industry.

Mr. Chair, members of the committee, thank you for the opportunity to appear before
you today and to present on this matter. My name is Ryan Rothschild.
I am speaking on behalf of a community of carnivorous plant growers
and enthusiasts across New Zealand.
I am joined by Ross Taylor, who has over 40 years of experience in this field,
including commercial cultivation and longstanding engagement with the
regulatory environment surrounding these plants.
We appreciate the committee's time, particularly given the wide range of
competing priorities you are tasked with addressing.
While our subject may appear niche at first glance, the issues we
raise go beyond a single plant group.
They speak directly to the integrity, consistency, and practical
workability of an important regulatory instrument, the Plant
Biosecurity Index.
At the center of our submission is a simple but significant problem.
The current Plant Biosecurity Index is incomplete in a way that
creates legal ambiguity, administrative inefficiency, and
unintended consequences for both regulators and the
public.
When the index was established in 1998, it was not compiled as a
comprehensive inventory of all plant species present in New Zealand at the
time. Instead, it relied largely on input from major commercial
growers and hobbyist societies, with a focus on
species actively traded in the market.
As a result, a substantial number of species, particularly those held
within smaller specialist communities, were never captured,
despite, in many cases, having been present in New Zealand for decades
prior.
Carnivorous plants are a clear example of this gap.
Based on the work we have undertaken over the past two years, the current
index includes approximately 178 species and
hybrids across 13 genre. However, the
evidence indicates that potentially upwards of
289 species across 14 genre were
already present in New Zealand prior to the index's inception,
but a great many were not included.
This is not a marginal discrepancy, but a massive structural shortfall.
In its current form, the Plant Biosecurity Index is not fit for purpose
as it relates to carnivorous plants.
That shortfall now gives rise to several serious and ongoing issues.
Firstly, there is the risk that there is unfair or
inconsistent enforcement. Individuals may be
cultivating and sharing plant species that have been present in
New Zealand for more than 30 years.
Yet, because those species are not listed, they may be exposed to
investigation or enforcement action.
In effect, the legality of their actions hinges not on biosecurity
risk, but whether a historically incomplete list happens to include the
species in question.
That is not a stable or equitable basis for regulation.
Secondly, there is a clear and avoidable cost to government.
The Ministry for Primary Industries is required, in some cases,
to investigate individual plants and collections to
determine whether the plants contained therein are lawfully or in New
Zealand or can be proved as having been present prior to
1998. They try to do this using the same
incomplete list, and the burden falls onto individual
collectors to prove the origins of plants held in their collection.
This is grossly unfair, is resource intensive for MPI,
time-consuming, and ultimately inefficient.
It diverts attention and funding away from higher risk biosecurity
priorities and places pressure on already constrained
systems. It also contributes to a loss of trust
between MPI and the people it services and investigates.
Thirdly, the current situation is contributing to the loss of
plant diversity within New Zealand.
Many longstanding private collections, some maintained over decades,
are not being propagated or shared.
Owners are understandably cautious about distributing material that may
expose them to legal risk or even admitting that they still hold it.
Over time, this leads to the quiet erosion of genetic diversity,
and that has been preserved in the country, often through the
dedication of private individuals rather than formal
institutions.
Fourthly, and importantly, it affects the credibility of the index itself.
A regulatory framework depends not only on its intent, but on
confidence in its accuracy and completeness.
Where it is widely understood within a sector that an underlying list is
materially incomplete, that confidence is diminished.
This has implications not only for compliance,
but also for the quality of decision-making that relies on the framework.
We also wish to highlight a practical barrier to resolving this issue under the
current system.
We have been advised that updating the index through standard processes may
involve costs of several thousand dollars for each species,
which would be payable by members of the community who seek to pursue
certain species being added to the list.
That model may be workable in large, commercially driven sectors
such as mainstream horticulture or agriculture.
However, it is not realistic for a small, largely non-commercial
community such as ours.
As a result, a known deficiency in the system remains unaddressed,
not because of lack of evidence, but because of lack of financial
capacity.With the list being so massively incomplete,
the burden is insurmountable for individuals or small groups of people with
a vested interest. Inevitably, this creates a black market
whilst penalizing those who are compliant with the law.
Despite these challenges, we have made substantial efforts to engage
constructively and in good faith.
Over the past two years, we have compiled a detailed and evidence-based body of
work to support the inclusion of these historically present species.
This includes records from botanical gardens, historical society
publications, and affidavits from individuals involved in the
original compilation of the lists, as well as longstanding growers
and operators.
Despite all of this work, our efforts have been mostly
unsuccessful because they say that the system puts the cost
of updating the list onto the consumer.
This position compounds all of the issues referred to
previously. MPI appear to have little appetite to
review their own tool, despite it being demonstratively inadequate and
incomplete.
Through this process, which has taken years, just eight additional species were
added to the index in 2023. Whilst that was a positive
step,
even this little success created a further issue.
Those species were simply placed in a requires assessment
category. This means that although they are now recognized,
they are not fully functional within the system.
For example, they can still not be readily imported due to the
absence of assigned import health standards.
From the perspective of those attempting to comply with the law in this country,
such bureaucracy simply penalizes meaningful and constructive
engagement, as essentially nothing is achieved despite full
cooperation with the statutory body and legislation.
This is not in keeping with the purpose and spirit of the Biosecurity Act, and
further erodes confidence by collectors in the system.
Thus, many avoid it at all costs and many end up
operating illegally through no real fault of their own, particularly if
they've held such plants for decades.
More broadly, we have been advised that there are thousands of species awaiting
assessment, with some being expected to remain in the queue for decades due
to resource constraints and prioritization towards larger
commercial interests. Despite this, the list is still being used
by government departments to justify decision-making, to inform
investigations and even prosecutions, and to limit or even
completely curtail trade.
This brings us to our recommendations.
We are not seeking to introduce new species into New Zealand,
nor are we advocating for any weakening of biosecurity protections.
We are fully in support and,
sorry. We fully support the purpose and importance of those protections.
Rather, we are asking for the system to accurately recognize what is already
here. Specifically, we ask that the committee supports a
process whereby the Plant Biosecurity Index is
updated to include species that can be demonstratively shown to
have been present in New Zealand prior to its inception, and that
those species are processed through the system
to have the necessary administrative steps completed to be
assigned the appropriate and functional status within the system,
including allocation of import health standards where applicable.
In doing so, we believe the committee would be supporting a more
coherent alignment between the Environmental Protection
Authority, the Plant Biosecurity Index, and the Ministry
for Primary Industries.
We ask that Parliament place the burden of ensuring that the list is fit for
purpose and up to date onto MPI and not
individual people, as doing so is in the best interests
of everyone and adds credibility and fitness for purpose to an existing
framework tool. It would also reduce unnecessary administrative
burden, improve regulatory clarity, and help prevent both
loss of biodiversity and the risk of unjust outcomes.
Finally,
we would emphasize that this submission is made in a constructive spirit.
Our community is committed to operating within the regulatory
framework and to supporting the objectives of New Zealand's biosecurity
system.
Our concern is that the current framework in the specific
respect of this does not fully reflect reality,
and that this gap can be and should be addressed.
Mr. Chair, members of the committee, thank you again for your time and
consideration. I will now conclude my remarks and invite any
questions you may have. If you have any questions, my friend and
colleague, Ross Taylor, will be happy to answer them.
He is one of New Zealand's longest-serving collectors of carnivorous plants.
He has spoken at numerous educational institutes and owns and operates
a specialist nursery devoted to carnivorous plants, and has
done so for almost 30 years. He was also one of the contributors
to the list back in the 1990s when the Ministry of Agriculture and
Fisheries was compiling the list.
Thanks very much, Ryan, and welcome, Ross.
After initial technical issues.
You're on mute.
So we'll go to first question with
Jo Luxton.
Oh, good morning. Thank you very much for your submission.

that it could be in the cost of tens of thousands of dollars.

Yes, they have. First of all, thank you
again for the opportunity to come, and I apologize for the technical issues.
I think it was to do with the fact that I was not connected as a panelist,
rather as an attendee. So hopefully you can hear me now.
Can you just confirm that you can?
Yes, we can
Okay, good. Thank you. Okay, the reason that it costs so much is because it's on a
per species basis. And so each species that gets added to the
list, there's a process that it has to go through.
And ordinarily, of course, when species get added, it's for commercial
intent.
So a major wholesale nursery, for example, might
choose to bring in a new species of hydrangea.
It's got massive commercial merit,
and therefore it has to be assessed for the New Zealand
ecosystem and also for the New Zealand Biosecurity Index.
And so the issue is that with all the plants that we grow,
they're not commercially viable. You wouldn't make anything out of them at all.
That's why nobody has.
But they are here nonetheless. They're already in New Zealand.
And so the problem is, is that because each one has to be
assessed, the cost is the same per species,
irrespective of whether it has commercial intent or not.
Okay. So when it is to be assessed, what is the
expense of assessing it? Is it
the process that it must go through?
Yes. There's a bureaucratic process that it goes through within MPI to get added to
the list, and also in theory there is. It doesn't always happen.
I'll talk about that in just a second.
But yes, there is a commercial process within MPI.
And also, there is some talk about it being assessed for the
environmental impact as well.
Okay.
And so those are two things. That being said, none of the species that are
currently on the list have ever been assessed.
They were added when the list was created.
And in fact, within the realm of carnivorous plants, there's a whole genera called
Pinguicula, of which, if you look at the list, there's probably about 100 or more
species on that list. Well, not one of those was ever assessed.
The only reason they're on the list is because there was a member of the
Carnivorous Plant Society that happened to be a chief botanist for MPI,
and he loved Pinguiculas, and so he added them all to the list.
That's the only reason that they're there.
Okay, now the fact of the matter is, is if he'd loved all the rest of the genera as
well, they'd all be there, but none of them are.
Thank you.
Steve,
is it a question of there being a broader public good and us having a
better track on
these exotic plant species and what their potential risks are
to biodiversity outside of any commercial use?
And therefore, are you alluding to there being a
need for some public investment in this?
Well, I think it's a fair question.
Whenever you bring a plant into a new ecosystem, there's always some level
of risk. It doesn't matter what the plant is.
With carnivorous plants, there's some risk.
Obviously they dwell in wetlands,
and so there have been other plants, for example, that have been
imported into New Zealand in the past that have become invasive.
And so I totally agree that there is a basis for the Biosecurity Index.
The fact of the matter remains that the list as it stands is incomplete.

and
